q: Which command is used to repair improperly shutdown, or otherwise potentially corrupt partitions?
exp: fsck (file system check) checks and repairs Linux filesystems. chkdsk/scandisk are Windows commands, fdisk is for partitioning.

q: Which Linux command can be used to determine the available space on local hard-disk partitions?
exp: df (disk free) shows available disk space on all mounted filesystems. 'free' shows RAM, 'du' shows directory sizes.

q: Which command line can be used to restart a running Linux system immediately?
exp: shutdown -r now restarts immediately. 'reboot -w' only writes to logs, 'halt -p' powers off but doesn't reboot.

q: Which Linux command can be used to create a new user account?
exp: useradd creates new user accounts with low-level control. adduser is more interactive. 'newuser' and 'mkuser' don't exist.

q: Which TCP/IP utility is BEST used to determine the hardware ethernet address of a network card?
exp: ifconfig (or ip link) displays network interfaces, MAC addresses, and IP configuration. arpwatch monitors ARP changes.

q: Which protocol/utility integrates Linux into a Windows network for sharing Windows files?
exp: SAMBA implements SMB protocol, allowing Linux to share files with Windows systems. NFS is for Unix/Linux networks.

q: Which program schedules particular jobs to run at particular times on the system?
exp: cron (daemon) executes scheduled tasks. crond runs in background; crontab edits jobs. 'scheduler' and 'outlook' don't exist in Linux.

q: What command gives executable permission to files named 'report'?
exp: chmod ugo+x report adds execute (+x) for user, group, others (ugo). u+x would only add it for owner.

q: LILO:
opts: is a tool used to boot Linux
exp: LILO = Linux Loader. All three statements are correct—it's a boot loader that loads the Linux kernel.

q: Maximum size of the swap partition to increase memory (kernel 2.1+)?
exp: In kernel 2.1+, maximum swap size increased to 2 GB (and later much higher). Earlier kernels had 128 MB limit.

q: What does OSS stand for?
exp: OSS = Open Source Software. Source code is freely available and can be modified and redistributed.

q: Which subdirectory contains the device files?
exp: /dev contains device files for hardware (disks, terminals). /bin has executables, /etc has configs, /home has user directories.

q: Which tool is used to check and repair Linux file systems?
exp: fsck checks and repairs filesystems. mkfs creates them, fdisk partitions disks, mount attaches filesystems.

q: Which tool is used to partition your hard drive?
exp: fdisk creates, modifies, and deletes disk partitions. mkfs formats partitions, fsck repairs them.

q: What command shows your current working directory location?
exp: pwd (print working directory) displays the full path of the current directory. 'which' finds command locations.

q: What vi editor command yanks (copies) a single line?
exp: yy copies (yanks) a line in vi. dd deletes a line. In command mode, these are basic navigation/edit commands.

q: The following are Linux distributions EXCEPT:
exp: FreeBSD is a Unix-like OS, not a Linux distribution. Linux distributions are based on the Linux kernel.

q: Which command displays the file type of /etc/passwd?
exp: file identifies file types (text, binary, etc). 'type' finds command locations. 'show' is not a standard command.

q: How do you concatenate file1.txt, file2.txt, and file4.txt?
exp: cat file1 file2 file4 displays files sequentially. touch creates empty files. vi opens editor.

q: What command finds the location of 'cat' and 'passwd'?
exp: which, find, and whereis all locate commands/files. 'which' is quickest for executables in PATH.

q: How can you add a new user to your Linux system?
exp: All three methods add users: useradd (low-level), adduser (interactive wrapper), linuxconf (text UI configuration tool).

q: Find files of type file (not directory) that end in .conf:
exp: find -type f -name '*.conf' searches for regular files ending in .conf. 'ls' doesn't have -type option.

q: Which directory stores templates for creating new user home directories?
exp: /etc/skel (skeleton) contains template files copied to new user home directories during creation.

q: What does FSF stand for?
exp: FSF = Free Software Foundation. Founded by Richard Stallman to promote free/open-source software and GNU project.

q: The change directory command to go to the parent directory is:
exp: cd .. moves to parent directory. cd ~ goes to home, cd - goes to previous directory, cd goes to home.

q: The BIOS looks for OS boot instructions in the:
exp: MBR (Master Boot Record) in the first sector of disk contains boot instructions. BIOS reads it to start the OS.

q: The default X.11 GUI environment in most open source OSes is:
exp: GNOME is the default desktop in most Linux distributions. KDE is an alternative. 'Desktop' and 'Ubuntu Desktop' refer to distributions.

q: Single command to remove directory ~/testbackup and all its contents:
exp: rm -rf recursively removes directory and all contents. rmdir only removes empty directories.

q: The mv command in Linux is used for:
exp: mv moves and/or renames files. cp copies keeping the original. rm deletes (not to trash, permanently).

q: Command to sort lines of a file in descending/reverse order:
exp: sort -r sorts in reverse (descending) order. 'sort' alone sorts ascending. 'at' schedules commands, 'sh' is shell.

q: vi editor command to save and quit the editing mode:
exp: wq saves and exits vi. :x also saves and exits. q! exits without saving. w only saves.

q: Linux executables files are found in the directory:
exp: /bin contains executable programs. /usr/bin has more. /etc has config files, /lib has libraries, /boot has kernel.

q: vi editor command to search a pattern in the forward direction:
exp: /pattern searches forward from cursor. ?pattern searches backward. # and * are not search operators in vi.

q: Which directory stores the log files in Linux/Unix?
exp: /var/log stores system and application logs. /var is for variable data like logs and temp files.

q: Which command removes packages AND their config files from Ubuntu Server?
exp: apt-get purge removes packages and config files. apt-get remove only removes packages but keeps configs.

q: Which of the following is NOT a default repository on Ubuntu Server 16?
exp: Community is not a default Ubuntu repository. Main, Restricted, Universe, Multiverse are the standard categories.

q: Four equal 20 GB disks [80 GB total]; after RAID install 40 GB remains. Which RAID type?
exp: RAID 1 (mirroring) uses 50% capacity for redundancy. 80 GB becomes 40 GB usable. RAID 5 would use about 75%.

q: You forgot the network config command. Which command finds it by keyword?
exp: apropos searches man pages by keyword. man opens specific manual. 'whatis' gives one-line descriptions.

q: Configuration file for Squid Proxy Services on Linux:
exp: squid.conf is the main Squid proxy configuration file, typically located in /etc/squid/squid.conf.

q: File to edit to configure a Secondary DNS Server:
exp: /etc/bind/named.conf.local (or .options for cache DNS) configures BIND DNS. Directory may be /etc/bind or /etc/bind9.

q: File to edit to configure a Cache DNS Server:
exp: /etc/bind/named.conf.options configures cache/recursive DNS settings. named.conf.local is for zones.

q: Useful package for testing and troubleshooting DNS issues:
exp: dnsutils provides DNS tools like nslookup and dig. Both apt and apt-get work; apt-get is the traditional form.

q: Command to restart the squid proxy after configuration:
exp: sudo systemctl restart squid.service restarts Squid. Service name is 'squid', not 'squid3' or 'squid.conf'.

q: File to configure the interface dhcpd listens on:
exp: /etc/default/isc-dhcp-server specifies which interfaces dhcpd listens on. dhcpd.conf configures DHCP settings.

q: Internet service that maps IP addresses to FQDNs:
exp: DNS (Domain Name System) translates between domain names and IP addresses. DHCP assigns IPs.

q: A name server that caches previous responses is termed:
exp: Cache DNS (resolver) stores previous query responses for faster retrieval. Primary serves zones; Secondary copies them.

q: IPV4: each octet can be any numeral between:
exp: Each octet (8 bits) in IPv4 ranges 0-255. 256 would overflow 8 bits (2^8 = 256 possible values: 0-255).

q: All are services that run on a Linux server EXCEPT:
exp: Ubuntu is an operating system/distribution. Apache, Email (various daemons), and Postfix (mail service) are services.

q: Popular Linux utility to search for text pattern matching in a file:
exp: grep searches for text patterns in files. locate finds files by name. find is more flexible but slower.

q: Which of the following is NOT a text editor in Linux/Unix?
exp: shell is a command interpreter, not a text editor. ed, cat (limited), and nano are text editors.

q: Command to list contents of directories:
exp: ls lists directory contents. tar archives files. dir is not standard (alias in some systems). lp prints.

q: Which user account has the highest privilege on Linux?
exp: root is the superuser (UID 0) with full system privileges. 'superuser' describes the role; 'root' is the actual username.

q: Command to change a user's password:
exp: passwd changes user passwords. useradd creates users. 'password' and 'passchange' are not standard commands.

q: What does GNU stand for?
exp: GNU = GNU's Not Unix. A recursive acronym for the free software project that created tools and the GPL license.

q: Command to restart a DNS service after configuration changes:
exp: sudo systemctl restart bind9.service restarts BIND DNS. Service name is 'bind9', not 'dns' or 'nameserver'.

q: Open Source Servers can be installed via:
exp: ISO image is a complete installation medium. Netinstall downloads during install. Kickstart automates installation.

q: Shell wildcard used to match a single character:
exp: ? matches one character. * matches any number. [...] matches character classes. {} expands alternatives.

q: Command to install bind9 (DNS) on Ubuntu Server:
exp: sudo apt-get install bind9 installs BIND DNS. Package name is 'bind9', not 'dns'.

q: What is known as a resource manager?
exp: The OS manages all system resources (CPU, memory, devices). The kernel is part of the OS. GUI is user interface.

q: Which of the following is NOT a system resource?
exp: The shell is a user interface/interpreter. Printers, network cards, memory, disk, and modems are physical/logical resources.

q: Which are the ways the OS allows access to system resources? (I=Efficient II=Abstract III=Reliable IV=Safe)
exp: OS provides all four: efficient (fast), abstract (simple APIs), reliable (consistent), and safe (protected) resource access.

q: Linux is a type of:
exp: Linux is a free, open-source operating system kernel. It's not DNS, GUI, or application software.

q: A typical Operating System is made up of the following EXCEPT:
exp: CPU is hardware, not part of the OS. Shell, kernel, and system libraries are OS components.

q: Linux supports how many forms of command input?
exp: Linux supports 2 main forms: shell/CLI and GUI. Some also count remote (network), making it 3 broadly.

q: Examples of application software are the following EXCEPT:
exp: Redhat is a Linux distribution (OS), not application software. Word, Spreadsheet, PowerPoint are applications.

q: When logging in, what is needed? (I=Username II=Picture III=Password)
exp: Username and password are required for login authentication. Pictures are not standard Linux login credentials.

q: What command is used to change one's password?
exp: passwd changes the user's password. Single word command 'passwd', not spaced like 'change password'.

q: The command to log out of a UNIX shell is:
exp: exit terminates the shell session. 'quit' and 'leave' are not standard. Ctrl+L clears the screen.

q: There are ……… file types in Linux:
exp: 3 main file types: ordinary files, directories, and special files (devices, sockets, pipes, links).

q: The following are file types EXCEPT:
exp: Devices are special files, not a separate category. Three main types: ordinary, directories, special (which includes devices).

q: The file type that is a list of other files is known as:
exp: Directories contain lists of filenames and inodes. Links reference other files. Special files are devices.

q: The file type that is a pointer to another file is:
exp: Links (symbolic/hard) point to other files. Directories contain file lists. Special files are devices.

q: The file type that contains text, data, or program information is:
exp: Ordinary files store text, data, or executable programs. Special files are devices. Directories list files.

q: Which file type provides applications with easy access to hardware devices?
exp: Device files (special files in /dev) provide interfaces to hardware. Apps access hardware through these files.

q: Which of the following directories is the root?
exp: / is the root directory at the top of the filesystem hierarchy. All others are subdirectories.

q: Which directory contains hardware devices?
exp: /dev contains device files for hardware (disks, terminals, printers). /tmp is temporary, /lib has libraries.

q: The command for listing the content of a directory is:
exp: ls lists directory contents. ps shows processes. 'Ps' and 'lw' are not standard commands.

q: ……… is a set of characters describing access rights.
exp: Permissions are the set of access rights (read, write, execute) for files/directories.

q: How many permission levels are there?
exp: 3 permission levels: owner (user), group, and others (world).

q: Which character denotes the OWNER permission level?
exp: u = user (owner). g = group. o = others. k is not used.

q: Which character denotes the GROUP permission level?
exp: g = group. u = user/owner. o = others. k is not used.

q: Which character denotes the OTHER (world) permission level?
exp: o = others (world). u = user. g = group. k is not used.

q: What command is used to change your current working directory?
exp: cd changes directory. ls lists files. ps shows processes. 'change' is not a standard command.

q: To view the content of a file, which command(s) are used?
exp: cat displays file content. Less/more are for paginated viewing. ls lists directories, cd changes them.

q: Which command is used to create a file?
exp: cat > filename creates a file (with input). touch creates empty files. mkdir creates directories.

q: The command used to delete a file is:
exp: rm removes files. Case matters in Linux; 'Rm' might not work (depends on shell). mv moves files.

q: The command used to move a file is:
exp: mv moves/renames files. rm deletes them. cat displays content. Case matters in commands.

q: Which option combined with 'shutdown' will halt the system?
exp: shutdown -h halts the system. -r reboots. -k only sends warning. -n prevents sync before shutdown.

q: Which option combined with 'shutdown' puts the system to sleep for a specified time?
exp: shutdown -s schedules shutdown for a time delay. -h halts. -r reboots. -k warns only.

q: Which shutdown option leaves the system multi-user with logins disabled?
exp: shutdown -k sends warning but doesn't actually shutdown. Single-user mode disables logins. This is a tricky question.

q: Which of the following is a valid piped command?
exp: Pipes (|) chain commands: output of one becomes input to next. $cat hello.txt|sort|uniq correctly pipes output.

q: Jobs can either be in:
exp: Jobs can run in foreground (user waits) or background (& suffix). pwd shows directory; not related to jobs.

q: The ……… operator creates concurrently executing processes that pass data to one another.
exp: Pipe (|) operator connects processes: output of one feeds to input of next. touch/echo are different commands.

q: Which statement about Unix processes is TRUE?
opts: Invoking a system utility may create one or more child processes
Few Unix processes have a unique process identifier
The pipe operator creates only one executing process
The foreground job has no control of the shell
exp: System utilities create child processes. All processes have unique PIDs. Pipes connect multiple processes.

q: Which statement about processes is FALSE?
opts: You can use pkill to kill processes by name instead of PID
top displays and constantly refreshes process statistics
On some systems, w is a non-interactive substitute for top
The foreground job may be suspended by pressing Ctrl-D
exp: Ctrl-D sends EOF. Ctrl-Z suspends. Ctrl-C terminates. pkill, top, and w all work as described.

q: If you have more than one job, you refer to them as %n where n is the job:
exp: %1, %2, etc. refer to job numbers (not names). 'jobs' command lists them with numbers.

q: An important process that is always present is the:
exp: Process ID 1 (init/systemd) is always present—the parent of all processes.

q: Which command provides an insecure mechanism for logging into remote machines?
exp: telnet logs in remotely but transmits credentials in cleartext (insecure). SSH encrypts the connection.

q: The ping command is used for:
opts: checking round-trip response time between machines
exp: ping checks if a host is reachable and measures response time. ls lists, touch creates, echo displays.

q: What commands show the list of users logged into a machine, their terminal, and login date?
exp: who and finger show logged-in users. telnet/grep/sudo/vi serve other purposes. ls lists files, cd changes dir.

q: What command lets you browse the web on a text-only terminal?
exp: lynx is a text-based web browser. Netscape is graphical (and obsolete). who/finger show users.

q: What does FTP stand for?
exp: FTP = File Transfer Protocol. Standard protocol for transferring files between computers over networks.

q: Which of these terminal commands will work when executed?
exp: traceroute (lowercase) is the correct command. Linux commands are case-sensitive.

q: Which command adds a document to a print queue?
exp: lpr adds jobs to print queue. lpq lists queue. lprm removes jobs. echo displays text.

q: CVS (Concurrent Versioning System) is:
exp: CVS manages version control for source code (now mostly replaced by Git). ls, cat, cd are shell commands.

q: Which of the following displays more information about commands?
exp: man shows detailed documentation. kill terminates processes. telnet connects remotely. mail sends messages.

q: What is the C++ compiler called?
exp: g++ is the GNU C++ compiler. cc is C, gg doesn't exist. CC may be used on some systems but g++ is standard.
